stem: The anterior pituitary produces five major hormones, each from a specific cell type. Which matching is MOST precisely and completely correct?
options: GH produced by somatotropes; TSH produced by thyrotropes; ACTH produced by corticotropes; FSH and LH produced by gonadotropes; prolactin produced by lactotropes
GH produced by somatotropes; TSH produced by thyrotropes; ACTH produced by corticotropes; FSH and LH produced by gonadotropes; prolactin produced by lactotrophs
GH produced by somatotropes; TSH produced by gonadotropes; ACTH produced by corticotropes; FSH and LH produced by thyrotropes; prolactin produced by lactotrophs
GH produced by somatotropes; TSH produced by thyrotropes; ACTH produced by gonadotropes; FSH and LH produced by corticotropes; prolactin produced by lactotrophs

stem: In the histological classification of anterior pituitary cells, cells are classified based on two specific staining characteristics. Which classification is MOST precisely stated?
options: Chromophobes and chromophils; chromophils are further divided into acidophils and basophils; hormone-secreting cells are chromophils; chromophobes are poorly staining non-secretory cells
Acidophils and basophils only; there is no chromophobe classification in the current histological system of pituitary cells
Chromophobes and chromophils only; chromophils are not further subdivided because all chromophils stain identically
Eosinophils and basophils; chromophobes are a third minor category of non-staining cells with no secretory function

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks which anterior pituitary cell type is the most abundant, accounting for about half of the anterior pituitary cells in preparation. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: Somatotropes, which produce growth hormone and are enormous cells making up about half of the anterior pituitary cells
Corticotropes, which produce ACTH and are the largest cells comprising approximately half of all anterior pituitary secretory cells
Lactotrophs, which produce prolactin and double in size during pregnancy making them the most numerous anterior pituitary cell type
Gonadotropes, which produce FSH and LH and are the most numerous cells because two hormones must be produced simultaneously

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): The student then asks about the staining characteristics of somatotropes. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: Somatotropes are acidophilic cells; they secrete growth hormone; they are enormous cells that comprise about half of the anterior pituitary cells
Somatotropes are basophilic cells; they secrete growth hormone; they are enormous cells that comprise about half of the anterior pituitary cells
Somatotropes are chromophobic cells; they secrete growth hormone; they are enormous cells that comprise about half of the anterior pituitary cells
Somatotropes are eosinophilic cells but classified as basophils; they secrete growth hormone; they make up about a quarter of the anterior pituitary cells

stem: Lactotrophs have a specific change during pregnancy. Which statement MOST precisely describes this?
options: Lactotrophs are acidophils that secrete prolactin; they double in size during pregnancy, making them the most numerous cells at that time
Lactotrophs are basophils that secrete prolactin; they double in number during pregnancy, making them the most numerous cells at that time
Lactotrophs are acidophils that secrete prolactin; they double in size during pregnancy, making them the second most numerous cells at that time
Lactotrophs are basophils that secrete prolactin; they increase by 30% in size during pregnancy due to oestrogen stimulation

stem: CASE STUDY: A student lists the basophilic anterior pituitary cells. Which combination is MOST precisely correct?
options: Corticotropes, gonadotropes, and thyrotropes are all basophilic cells
Corticotropes, somatotropes, and thyrotropes are basophilic cells
Gonadotropes, lactotrophs, and thyrotropes are basophilic cells
Corticotropes, gonadotropes, and lactotrophs are basophilic cells

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q6): A student asks about gonadotropes specifically. Which statement is MOST precisely correct?
options: Gonadotropes are basophilic cells that produce FSH and luteinising hormone (LH); they constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells
Gonadotropes are acidophilic cells that produce FSH and LH; they constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells
Gonadotropes are basophilic cells that produce FSH and LH; they constitute 5-10% of the anterior pituitary cells
Gonadotropes are chromophobic cells that produce FSH and LH; they constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells

stem: Thyrotropes represent a specific percentage of the anterior pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Thyrotropes represent less than 10% of the anterior pituitary cells; they are basophilic in staining characteristics; they secrete thyroid-stimulating hormones (TSH)
Thyrotropes represent less than 10% of the anterior pituitary cells; they are acidophilic in staining characteristics; they secrete thyroid-stimulating hormones (TSH)
Thyrotropes represent 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells; they are basophilic in staining characteristics; they secrete thyroid-stimulating hormones (TSH)
Thyrotropes represent less than 10% of the anterior pituitary cells; they are chromophobic in staining characteristics; they secrete thyroid-stimulating hormones (TSH)

stem: The 'Others' category of anterior pituitary cells is mentioned. Which statement MOST precisely describes them?
options: More recently, specialised cells called folliculostellate cells have been identified; they produce cytokine IL-6; they exhibit autocrine or paracrine actions; they consist of supporting cells (pituicytes)
More recently, specialised cells called folliculostellate cells have been identified; they produce cytokine IL-12; they exhibit endocrine actions; they consist of supporting cells (pituicytes)

More recently, specialised cells called folliculostellate cells have been identified; they produce cytokine IL-6; they exhibit autocrine or paracrine actions; they consist of sustentacular cells (Sertoli-like cells)
More recently, specialised cells called parastellate cells have been identified; they produce cytokine IL-6; they exhibit paracrine actions only; they consist of supporting cells (pituicytes)

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is asked about the blood supply to the anterior pituitary. Which description is MOST completely and precisely correct?
options: Blood supply to the anterior pituitary comes from the superior, middle, and inferior hypophyseal arteries â all branches of the internal carotid artery; the superior hypophyseal artery forms a primary capillary network in the median eminence; this network recombines to create a long portal vein draining down the pituitary stalk to the adenohypophysis; a breakup generates a secondary capillary network; hormones are transported via the hypothalami-hypophyseal portal system

Blood supply to the anterior pituitary comes from the superior and inferior hypophyseal arteries only â branches of the external carotid artery; the superior hypophyseal artery forms a primary network in the median eminence; this creates a short portal vein draining to the adenohypophysis; a breakup generates a secondary capillary network

Blood supply to the anterior pituitary comes from the superior hypophyseal artery only â a branch of the internal carotid artery; it forms a primary capillary network in the anterior pituitary directly without a portal system

Blood supply to the anterior pituitary comes from the superior, middle, and inferior hypophyseal arteries â all branches of the vertebral artery; the superior hypophyseal artery forms a primary capillary network at the infundibular process

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q10): Which organ is described as the MOST vascularised of all mammalian tissues?
options: The anterior pituitary is the most vascularised of all mammalian tissues
The median eminence is the most vascularised of all mammalian tissues
The hypothalamus is the most vascularised of all mammalian tissues
The neurohypophysis is the most vascularised of all mammalian tissues

stem: The hypothalami-hypophyseal portal system serves a specific purpose. Which description is MOST precisely stated?
options: The portal system ensures that significant amounts of neurohormones are transported from the median eminence to their target cells in the anterior pituitary, while insignificant amounts reach the peripheral circulation
The portal system ensures that all neurohormones produced in the hypothalamus reach equal concentrations in both the anterior pituitary and the peripheral circulation
The portal system ensures that neurohormones bypass the anterior pituitary and are delivered directly to the target organs such as the thyroid and adrenal glands
The portal system ensures that significant amounts of neurohormones are delivered to the peripheral circulation, while insignificant amounts reach the anterior pituitary target cells

stem: CASE STUDY: The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply a specific region of the pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply the neurohypophysis and some direct branches come from the superior hypophyseal arteries
The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply the adenohypophysis and some direct branches come from the superior hypophyseal arteries
The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply only the posterior pituitary, while the anterior pituitary receives exclusive supply from the superior hypophyseal artery
The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply the median eminence and some direct branches come from the superior hypophyseal arteries

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q13): The student asks how the final drainage of the pituitary portal system occurs. What is MOST precisely stated?
options: A breakup occurs to generate a secondary capillary network that finally drains as the hypophyseal vein from where hormones are transported to other parts of the body
A breakup occurs to generate a secondary capillary network that finally drains as the portal vein from where hormones are transported to other parts of the body
A breakup occurs to generate a tertiary capillary network that finally drains as the hypophyseal vein from where hormones are transported to other parts of the body
A breakup occurs to generate a secondary venous sinus that finally drains as the jugular vein from where hormones are transported to other parts of the body

stem: The posterior pituitary consists of specific cell types and structures. Which statement is MOST precisely correct?
options: The posterior pituitary consists of the neural stalk (infundibular stem) and pars nervosa (infundibular process); it consists of numerous blood vessels, non-myelinated nerve fibres of supraoptic (SON) and paraventricular (PVN) nuclei cells, mast cells, and supporting cells (pituicytes)

The posterior pituitary consists of the neural stalk (infundibular stem) and pars nervosa (infundibular process); it consists of myelinated nerve fibres of SON and PVN cells, blood vessels, mast cells, and supporting cells (pituicytes)

The posterior pituitary consists of pars tuberalis and pars nervosa; it consists of numerous blood vessels, non-myelinated nerve fibres of SON and PVN cells, mast cells, and supporting cells (pituicytes)

The posterior pituitary consists of the neural stalk and pars intermedia; it consists of numerous blood vessels, non-myelinated nerve fibres of SON and PVN cells, mast cells, and supporting cells (pituicytes)

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about the description of the posterior lobe of the pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The posterior lobe of the pituitary is more like an extension of the ventral hypothalamus; the axons of the magnocellular cells of SON and PVN terminate primarily in the posterior pituitary lobe where secretory granules containing antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin are in close proximity with adjacent capillaries

The posterior lobe of the pituitary is more like an extension of the dorsal hypothalamus; the axons of the magnocellular cells of SON and PVN terminate in the anterior pituitary lobe where secretory granules containing ADH and oxytocin are stored

The posterior lobe of the pituitary is more like an extension of the ventral hypothalamus; the axons of the parvocellular cells of SON and PVN terminate in the posterior pituitary lobe where secretory granules containing ADH and oxytocin are stored

The posterior lobe of the pituitary is more like an extension of the dorsal thalamus; the axons of the magnocellular cells terminate in the median eminence where ADH and oxytocin are released into the portal system

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q16): How are secretions from the posterior pituitary lobe released?
options: Secretions occur when nerve impulses originate from the cells in the hypothalamus; the relationship between the pituitary and hypothalamus is called the hypothalamic-hypophyseal nervous tract
Secretions occur when hormones from the anterior pituitary diffuse backward through the portal system to stimulate posterior pituitary cells
Secretions occur when hypothalamic releasing hormones from the median eminence travel down the portal vein to the posterior pituitary
Secretions occur when systemic blood concentrations of ADH and oxytocin fall below threshold, triggering autoregulatory release from the posterior pituitary

stem: The embryological origin of the anterior pituitary is specifically described. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: The anterior pituitary develops from ectoderm, arising from the pharyngeal epithelium, from an upward growth called Rathke's pouch, which migrates to contact the neurohypophysis
The anterior pituitary develops from endoderm, arising from the pharyngeal epithelium, from an upward growth called Rathke's pouch, which migrates to contact the neurohypophysis
The anterior pituitary develops from ectoderm, arising from the oral epithelium, from a downward growth called Rathke's pouch, which migrates to contact the neurohypophysis
The anterior pituitary develops from mesoderm, arising from the pharyngeal epithelium, from an upward growth called Rathke's pouch, which migrates to contact the neurohypophysis

stem: CASE STUDY: During embryological development, a portion of Rathke's pouch tends to decline. What happens to this portion in humans versus other species?
options: A portion of the upward growth tends to decline to form the intermediate lobe; in some species this lobe remains intact; in humans it tends to degenerate; it is believed to be incorporated into the anterior portion and develops the capacity to produce POMC and hypothalamus develops ACTH
A portion of the upward growth tends to decline to form the posterior lobe; in some species this lobe remains intact; in humans it tends to degenerate; it is incorporated into the posterior pituitary
A portion of the upward growth tends to decline to form the intermediate lobe; in all species this lobe remains intact; in humans it enlarges progressively throughout adult life
A portion of the upward growth tends to decline to form the pars tuberalis; in humans this structure is rudimentary; it is believed to develop capacity for prolactin production

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q19): The intermediate lobe produces specific molecules when incorporated into the anterior portion. What does it develop the capacity to produce?
options: Proopiomelanocortin (POMC) and the hypothalamus develops adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)
Proopiomelanocortin (POMC) and the anterior pituitary develops adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)
Proopiomelanocortin (POMC) and the pituitary stalk develops adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)
Pro-opioid-melanocortin (POMC) and the hypothalamus develops anti-corticotropic hormone (ACTH)

stem: The posterior pituitary has a different embryological origin than the anterior pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The posterior pituitary and hypothalamus develop as a downward diverticulum from the neuroepithelium of the base of the brain, in the floor of the third ventricle
The posterior pituitary and hypothalamus develop as a downward diverticulum from the neuroepithelium of the base of the brain, in the roof of the fourth ventricle
The posterior pituitary and hypothalamus develop as an upward diverticulum from the neuroepithelium of the base of the brain, in the floor of the third ventricle
The posterior pituitary and hypothalamus develop as a downward diverticulum from the neuroepithelium of the base of the brain, in the lateral wall of the third ventricle

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse educator asks students about the staining characteristics of corticotropes. Which answer is MOST precisely correct?
options: Corticotropes are basophilic cells; they produce ACTH and other related peptides; they are also basophilic other pituitary cells; they comprise approximately 15-20% of anterior pituitary cells
Corticotropes are acidophilic cells; they produce ACTH and other related peptides; they comprise approximately 15-20% of anterior pituitary cells
Corticotropes are chromophobic cells; they produce ACTH and other related peptides; they comprise approximately 15-20% of anterior pituitary cells
Corticotropes are basophilic cells; they produce ACTH only; they are the smallest cells in the anterior pituitary; they comprise approximately 5% of anterior pituitary cells

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q22): The nurse asks students what POMC is and from which cells it is produced. Which answer is MOST accurate?
options: POMC (proopiomelanocortin) is a precursor molecule produced from corticotropes and from cells of the intermediate lobe incorporated into the anterior portion; it gives rise to ACTH among other peptides
POMC (proopiomelanocortin) is produced exclusively from the posterior pituitary magnocellular cells; it is the precursor for ADH and oxytocin
POMC (proopiomelanocortin) is a precursor molecule produced exclusively from gonadotropes; it gives rise to FSH, LH, and related glycoprotein hormones
POMC (proopiomelanocortin) is produced from the intermediate lobe only; once the intermediate lobe degenerates in humans, POMC production ceases completely

stem: The anterior pituitary is described as the most vascularised tissue. Which specific vessels provide its blood supply?
options: Superior, middle, and inferior hypophyseal arteries â all branches of the internal carotid artery
Superior and inferior hypophyseal arteries â branches of the external carotid artery
Superior, middle, and inferior hypophyseal arteries â branches of the vertebral artery
Superior hypophyseal artery only â a direct branch of the internal carotid artery

stem: CASE STUDY: A clinical student describes the step-by-step blood flow through the hypothalami-hypophyseal portal system. Which sequence is MOST precisely correct?
options: Superior hypophyseal artery â primary capillary network in median eminence â long portal vein draining down pituitary stalk â adenohypophysis â secondary capillary network â hypophyseal vein â systemic circulation
Superior hypophyseal artery â primary capillary network in anterior pituitary â long portal vein ascending to median eminence â secondary capillary network â hypophyseal vein
Inferior hypophyseal artery â primary capillary network in median eminence â long portal vein â posterior pituitary â secondary capillary network â hypophyseal vein
Superior hypophyseal artery â primary capillary network in median eminence â short portal vein â adenohypophysis â secondary capillary network â portal vein

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q25): A student incorrectly states: 'Significant amounts of neurohormones reach the peripheral circulation.' The correct statement from the notes is:
options: The portal system ensures significant amounts of neurohormones reach target cells in the anterior pituitary while insignificant amounts reach the peripheral circulation
The portal system ensures equal amounts of neurohormones reach both the anterior pituitary and the peripheral circulation
The portal system ensures all neurohormones bypass the anterior pituitary and are delivered directly to the peripheral circulation
The portal system ensures significant amounts of neurohormones reach the peripheral circulation while insignificant amounts reach the anterior pituitary

stem: The anterior pituitary hormone to be discussed in detail in the noted chapter is:
options: Growth hormone (GH), while TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, and prolactin will be discussed in other sections as they affect control of their respective target hormones
TSH, while growth hormone, ACTH, FSH, LH, and prolactin will be discussed in other sections
ACTH, while growth hormone, TSH, FSH, LH, and prolactin will be discussed in other sections
Prolactin, while growth hormone, TSH, ACTH, FSH, and LH will be discussed in other sections

stem: CASE STUDY: A histology student asks what staining method is used to classify anterior pituitary cells. Which is MOST precisely derived from the notes?
options: Cells are classified into two main types by the basis of their staining properties with acidic and basic dyes; chromophobes and chromophils; chromophils are acidophils and basophils; identification uses immunocytochemistry, specific immunostaining techniques, and electron microscopy
Cells are classified into three main types by haematoxylin and eosin staining alone; identification uses only light microscopy
Cells are classified by periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) staining only; acidophils are PAS-positive while basophils are PAS-negative
Cells are classified by their granule content using transmission electron microscopy only; no light microscopy classification exists

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q28): A student states: 'Chromophobes are hormone-secreting cells.' The most accurate correction is:
options: Chromophobes are poorly staining cells in the anterior pituitary; hormone-secreting cells are chromophils (acidophils and basophils), not chromophobes
Chromophobes are the only hormone-secreting cells; chromophils are supporting cells that do not secrete hormones
Chromophobes and chromophils are both hormone-secreting cells; the distinction is based on staining intensity only
Chromophobes secrete only chromogranin; chromophils secrete all anterior pituitary hormones

stem: The folliculostellate cells in the anterior pituitary produce a specific interleukin. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Folliculostellate cells produce cytokine IL-6
Folliculostellate cells produce cytokine IL-1
Folliculostellate cells produce cytokine IL-12
Folliculostellate cells produce cytokine IL-2

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse asks about the nerve fibre characteristics within the posterior pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The posterior pituitary consists of non-myelinated nerve fibres from supraoptic (SON) and paraventricular (PVN) nuclei cells
The posterior pituitary consists of myelinated nerve fibres from supraoptic (SON) and paraventricular (PVN) nuclei cells
The posterior pituitary consists of non-myelinated nerve fibres from only the paraventricular nuclei cells
The posterior pituitary consists of non-myelinated nerve fibres from hypothalamic parvocellular neurosecretory cells

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q31): The student asks what ADH is also called. Which is MOST precisely stated from standard endocrinology nomenclature consistent with the notes?
options: ADH is also called antidiuretic hormone and vasopressin; it is stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe
ADH is also called antidiuretic hormone and aldosterone; it is stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe
ADH is also called antidiuretic hormone and atrial natriuretic peptide; it is produced in the posterior pituitary
ADH is also called antidiuretic hormone only; there is no second name mentioned for ADH in standard endocrinology

stem: The Rathke's pouch is a specific embryological structure. What is it and what does it do?
options: Rathke's pouch is the upward growth of pharyngeal epithelium that migrates to contact the neurohypophysis during development of the anterior pituitary
Rathke's pouch is the downward growth of neuroepithelium that migrates to contact the pharyngeal ectoderm during development of the posterior pituitary
Rathke's pouch is the lateral growth of oral endoderm that migrates upward to fuse with the descending diverticulum from the floor of the third ventricle
Rathke's pouch is the upward growth of mesodermal tissue that migrates to contact the neuroepithelium during pituitary gland organogenesis

stem: CASE STUDY: A radiologist describes a pituitary MRI showing an enlarged pituitary in a pregnant patient. The nurse explains this is physiological. The cells responsible for this enlargement are:
options: Lactotrophs which double in size during pregnancy, making them the most numerous cells at that time
Somatotropes which double in number during pregnancy due to increased GH demand
Gonadotropes which increase in number during pregnancy due to rising FSH and LH requirements
Corticotropes which double in size during pregnancy due to increased cortisol demand

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q34): The radiologist then asks which anterior pituitary cells are acidophilic. Which is the COMPLETE and correct list?
options: Somatotropes and lactotrophs are acidophilic cells
Somatotropes and corticotropes are acidophilic cells
Somatotropes, lactotrophs, and thyrotropes are acidophilic cells
Only somatotropes are acidophilic; lactotrophs are basophilic

stem: The relationship between the hypothalamus and posterior pituitary through axonal projections is named. What is this relationship called?
options: The hypothalamic-hypophyseal nervous tract
The hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system
The infundibular-hypophyseal nervous tract
The hypothalamic-neurohypophyseal portal tract

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about the specialised vascular relationship between the hypothalamus and the anterior pituitary. What is this system specifically called?
options: The hypothalami-hypophyseal portal system
The hypothalamic-hypophyseal nervous tract
The hypothalami-anterior pituitary portal system
The infundibulo-hypophyseal portal system

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q37): A student confuses the blood supply of the neurohypophysis with that of the adenohypophysis. Which statement correctly identifies what supplies the neurohypophysis?
options: The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply the neurohypophysis, with some direct branches from the superior hypophyseal arteries
The superior hypophyseal artery exclusively supplies the neurohypophysis via the portal system
The inferior hypophyseal artery exclusively supplies the neurohypophysis with no contribution from superior branches
The middle cerebral artery supplies the neurohypophysis via perforating branches through the sella turcica

stem: The immunocytochemistry technique is used in addition to standard staining for pituitary cell identification. Why is this additional technique needed?
options: Because the specificity of hormone secretion requires more than morphological staining to identify the specific hormones produced by each cell type; immunocytochemistry uses specific immunostaining and electron microscopy for this purpose
Because chromophobes cannot be identified by standard staining and require immunostaining exclusively for detection and quantification
Because electron microscopy is the only method that can distinguish between acidophils and basophils reliably
Because PAS staining is insufficient for identifying basophilic cells and immunostaining is required for all basophil subtypes

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks what portion of the pituitary some people may sometimes regard as the most superior part of the neurohypophysis. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Some people may sometimes regard part of the median eminence where the neural stalk attaches as the most superior part of the neurohypophysis
Some people may sometimes regard the pars tuberalis where the neural stalk attaches as the most superior part of the neurohypophysis
Some people may sometimes regard the pars intermedia where the neural stalk attaches as the most superior part of the neurohypophysis
Some people may sometimes regard the infundibular process where the neural stalk attaches as the most superior part of the neurohypophysis

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q40): The student then asks about Rathke's pouch specifically. In which direction does it grow and what does it migrate toward?
options: Rathke's pouch is an upward growth of pharyngeal epithelium that migrates to contact the neurohypophysis
Rathke's pouch is a downward growth of neuroepithelium that migrates to contact the pharyngeal epithelium
Rathke's pouch is an upward growth of oral endoderm that migrates to contact the floor of the third ventricle
Rathke's pouch is a lateral growth of branchial arch mesoderm that migrates to form the anterior pituitary stroma

stem: Corticotropes produce ACTH and other related peptides. What additional feature is noted about their staining in relation to other anterior pituitary basophilic cells?
options: Corticotropes are basophilic; they produce ACTH and other related peptides; they are also basophilic other pituitary cells that produce peptides
Corticotropes are basophilic; they produce ACTH only; no other related peptides are produced by this cell type
Corticotropes are acidophilic, unlike other ACTH-producing cells which are basophilic; this makes them unique among anterior pituitary cell types
Corticotropes are chromophobic, unlike gonadotropes and thyrotropes which are both basophilic; this staining difference reflects their different embryological origin

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient develops panhypopituitarism. The nurse identifies that one specific pituitary cell type is probably the least affected given its high abundance. Which cell type?
options: Somatotropes, because they comprise about half of the anterior pituitary cells and are the most numerous cell type
Thyrotropes, because they are less than 10% of cells and their small number makes them resistant to generalised damage
Lactotrophs, because they double in size during pregnancy making them the largest and most resilient cells
Gonadotropes, because FSH and LH production occurs in the same cell making them functionally redundant

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q43): A student asks which cell type constitutes 10-15% of the anterior pituitary. The MOST precise answer is:
options: Gonadotropes constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells
Thyrotropes constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells
Corticotropes constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells
Lactotrophs constitute 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells

stem: The posterior pituitary specifically stores two hormones in secretory granules. Which pair is MOST precisely stated?
options: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin are stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and prolactin are stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and growth hormone are stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and corticotropin are stored in secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is asked what specific type of cells the 'pituicytes' are in the posterior pituitary. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Pituicytes are the supporting cells of the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis)
Pituicytes are the hormone-secreting magnocellular neurons of the posterior pituitary
Pituicytes are the chromophobic non-secretory cells of the anterior pituitary
Pituicytes are the folliculostellate cells of the anterior pituitary that produce IL-6

stem: The anterior pituitary develops from which embryological germ layer?
options: Ectoderm â arising from the pharyngeal epithelium as Rathke's pouch
Endoderm â arising from the pharyngeal epithelium as Rathke's pouch
Mesoderm â arising from the pharyngeal mesenchyme as Rathke's pouch
Neuroectoderm â arising from the floor of the third ventricle as Rathke's pouch

stem: GH is produced by somatotropes; TSH is produced by thyrotropes; ACTH is produced by corticotropes; FSH and LH are produced by gonadotropes; prolactin is produced by _______________.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Somatotropes are _______________ cells that produce growth hormone and are enormous, comprising about half of the anterior pituitary cells.' One staining characteristic must be supplied.
Lactotrophs are acidophils that secrete prolactin; they _______________ in size during pregnancy, making them the most numerous cells at that time.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Thyrotropes represent less than _______________% of the anterior pituitary cells and are basophilic in staining characteristics.' One precise percentage must be supplied.
The anterior pituitary is the most _______________ of all mammalian tissues.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The superior hypophyseal artery forms a primary capillary network in the _______________ eminence.' One precise anatomical structure must be supplied.
The specialised vascular relationship between the hypothalamus and the anterior pituitary is called the hypothalami-_______________ portal system.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'A breakup occurs to generate a secondary capillary network that finally drains as the _______________ vein.' One precise vessel name must be supplied.

The portal system ensures that significant amounts of neurohormones are transported from the median eminence to their target cells in the anterior pituitary, while _______________ amounts reach the peripheral circulation.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'The anterior pituitary develops from ectoderm, arising from the pharyngeal epithelium, from an upward growth called _______________'s pouch.' One proper name must be supplied.
The posterior pituitary and hypothalamus develop as a downward diverticulum from the neuroepithelium of the base of the brain, in the floor of the _______________ ventricle.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The intermediate lobe is incorporated into the anterior portion and develops the capacity to produce _______________ (POMC).' The full name of this molecule must be supplied.

The posterior pituitary consists of non-myelinated nerve fibres of supraoptic (SON) and paraventricular (PVN) nuclei cells, numerous blood vessels, mast cells, and supporting cells called _______________.

CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'The relationship between the pituitary and hypothalamus via axonal projections carrying ADH and oxytocin is called the hypothalamic-_______________ nervous tract.' One precise term must be supplied.
Folliculostellate cells are specialised cells that produce cytokine _______________-6 and exhibit autocrine or paracrine actions.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Gonadotropes are _______________ cells that produce FSH and luteinising hormone, constituting 10-15% of the anterior pituitary cells.' One staining characteristic must be supplied.
The histological classification of anterior pituitary cells identifies chromophobes and chromophils; chromophils are further divided into _______________ and basophils.
CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The superior hypophyseal artery is a branch of the _______________ carotid artery.' One precise adjective must be supplied.
The posterior pituitary is more like an extension of the _______________ hypothalamus.
CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'In some species, the intermediate lobe remains intact; in humans, it tends to _______________.' One precise verb from the notes must be supplied.
Secretory granules in the posterior pituitary lobe containing ADH and oxytocin are in close proximity with adjacent _______________ when nerve impulses cause secretion.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The anterior pituitary hormone discussed in detail in the chapter is _______________ hormone.' One precise hormone name must be supplied.
The inferior and middle hypophyseal arteries supply the _______________, and some direct branches come from the superior hypophyseal arteries.
